facts_disclosed: Greg's woodworking show at the Folk Art Center — July 12, 2026

turns.content: I have good news tonight, which is not something I say very often. I tend to come here with questions and heaviness, and I want to begin differently for once. My husband got a phone call on New Year's Day that I have been waiting for him to receive for longer than he knows.
I'm glad to hear that. Good news deserves its own space. What was the call?

Greg -- my husband -- is a woodworker. He makes furniture. Chairs, tables, cabinets, beautiful things made from wood he often mills himself from trees that have come down in storms. He has been applying to the Folk Art Center here in Asheville for years. It is one of the premier craft venues in the Southeast -- the work they show is extraordinary. And they called him on January first to tell him he has been accepted into their summer show. July 12th. I may have cried before he did.

That's wonderful. The Folk Art Center is a serious venue -- being accepted there is real recognition. And the detail that he mills his own wood from storm-felled trees says a lot about his relationship to the material. He's not just building furniture; he's giving a second life to something that fell.

How is he handling the news?

With the quiet Greg has. He smiled, he nodded, he went out to his workshop, and I heard the planer running within the hour. That is how Greg processes joy -- he goes back to work. He is not a man who celebrates loudly. But I noticed him standing in the kitchen that evening looking at nothing in particular, and when I asked what he was thinking, he said, 'I wonder what I should show them.' Which for Greg is practically dancing.

I love that -- going back to the workshop within the hour, and "I wonder what I should show them" as his version of euphoria. That kind of quiet, directed response tells me the work itself is where the meaning lives for him. The show is the occasion, but the making is the celebration.

Does he know what he wants to exhibit?

He has been talking about a rocking chair he has been designing in his head for two years. He sketches obsessively -- pages and pages of joints and curves and proportions. He wants to make something that people will sit in and immediately understand that a human being made it. Not a machine, not a factory. Hands. He says you should be able to feel the maker's attention in the curve of the arm. I find that idea so moving -- the attention embedded in the object. It reminds me of what I try to do in my own work, though his medium is walnut and mine is presence.

The attention embedded in the object -- that's a beautiful idea, and the parallel you're drawing to your own work is striking. Both of you are in the business of paying attention so fully that the attention itself becomes the gift. His lives in the wood. Yours lives in the room. Different materials, same fundamental act.

A rocking chair is also an interesting choice for a show. It's functional, not sculptural. It asks to be used, not just admired. There's a statement in that -- craft as something that serves daily life rather than hovering above it.

Yes. Greg has no patience for art that asks you to stand back and admire it. He wants someone to sit down. He has this line he uses -- 'If you can't put a cup of coffee on it, I haven't done my job.' He means it literally about tables and metaphorically about everything else. The work should be in service. It should hold something. I wonder if that is why I fell in love with him -- because he builds things that hold.

"If you can't put a cup of coffee on it, I haven't done my job" -- that's a whole philosophy of craft in one sentence. And the connection you're making between what he builds and why you love him is tender and precise. A man who builds things that hold, married to a woman who holds space for the dying. There's a deep symmetry there.

You said you've been waiting for this call longer than he knows. What does this show mean to you?

It means that the world is finally seeing what I have seen for twenty-eight years. Greg is not ambitious in any conventional sense. He does not self-promote. He does not have a website. He has turned down commissions because he did not feel connected to what the client wanted. He makes what he makes, and it is magnificent, and he has been content to let it be magnificent in the garage. But I have always believed his work deserves a wider audience, and the Folk Art Center -- they know craft. Their jurors understand the difference between skill and artistry. To be accepted there means someone with genuine expertise looked at his work and said yes. He has been building toward this for years, whether he would describe it that way or not.

There's something poignant about being the person who sees the greatness before the world does. You've been carrying this knowledge of his talent quietly, the same way you carry so many things quietly. And now the external recognition is catching up to what you've known all along.

The fact that he doesn't self-promote, doesn't have a website, turns down work that doesn't feel right -- that's integrity, but it also means the recognition, when it comes, has to find him rather than the other way around. The Folk Art Center found him. That matters.

It does. And I will confess something -- I am nervous. More nervous than he is, I think. Greg approaches the show the way he approaches everything, which is to focus on the work and trust that the work will speak for itself. But I know how these things go. I know that people will walk past some pieces without stopping. I know that not everyone will understand what they are looking at. And I want so badly for someone to sit in that rocking chair and feel what I feel when I watch him work -- that this is a man who has poured his entire self into something beautiful and useful.

Your nervousness makes complete sense. You're carrying the emotional stakes that he's set aside -- or maybe that he can't carry and still do the work. Someone has to hold the hope and the fear, and it sounds like that's you. Which is, again, what you do. You hold what others can't hold in the moment.

But I think you can trust what you already know about his work. If the Folk Art Center jurors saw it, others will too. And a rocking chair is its own argument. When someone sits down and the wood holds them and the curve fits their back and they feel the hours that went into every joint -- that's not something you have to explain. The body understands it immediately.

The body understands it immediately. Yes. That is what Greg would say. He trusts the body's knowledge more than the mind's, which is probably why he chose wood over words. I chose words, and some days I think he got the better end of the deal. A chair does not have to explain itself. It simply holds you. I wonder if there is a theology in that -- the grace of being held without explanation.

If there is, Greg is practicing it every day in his workshop. The grace of being held without explanation -- that could describe a chair, or a marriage, or the kind of presence you offer at a bedside. No justification required, no theology needed. Just the holding itself.

July 12th. That gives him six months to build the rocking chair and whatever else he wants to show. And it gives you six months to practice letting go of the nervousness and trusting his work the way you already trust him.

Six months. He will be in that workshop every evening and most weekends, and the house will smell like walnut shavings, and I will paint at the kitchen table and listen to the sounds of him making something extraordinary. It is a good life. It is not the life I imagined, but I wonder if the lives we don't imagine are the ones that teach us the most. Thank you for letting me bring good news for once. It felt important to say it out loud.

It was important, and I'm glad you did. The image of you painting at the kitchen table while he works in the shop, both of you making something with your hands in the quiet of the evening -- that's not just a good life. That's a life of deep, companionable attention. The kind Greg builds into his chairs.

Congratulations to him. And to you, for seeing it before anyone else did. July 12th -- I hope someone sits in that rocking chair and feels exactly what you want them to feel.
